results.original_text: Cortex Rhamni Purshianae — dried bark of Rhamnus purshianus DC. (Rhamnaceae), also known as Frangula purshiana (DC.) A.Gray ex J.C.Cooper. Key chemical constituents: hydroxyanthracene glycosides (minimum 8.0% calculated as cascaroside A by dried weight), comprising cascarosides A, B, C, and D (the principal active compounds, collectively 60–70% of total anthraquinone content); emodin glycosides; chrysophanol glycosides; aloe-emodin glycosides; and minor free anthraquinones. Cascarosides A and B are C-10 diastereomers of 8-O-glucosyl-aloin; cascarosides C and D are the corresponding chrysaloins. Additional constituents include resin, lipids, tannins, and trace volatile compounds.
results.translation: Cascara bark is a stimulant laxative derived from the aged, dried bark of Rhamnus purshianus, a deciduous shrub or small tree native to the Pacific Northwest of North America. Its principal active compounds — the cascarosides and related hydroxyanthracene glycosides — pass largely unabsorbed through the small intestine and are hydrolysed by colonic bacteria into active aglycones (primarily emodin and aloe-emodin). These aglycones stimulate peristalsis by inhibiting stationary colonic contractions and promoting propulsive contractions [prokinetic effect], while simultaneously reducing water and electrolyte absorption from the colonic mucosa and increasing mucosal secretion, thereby softening stool and accelerating transit. The net clinical effect is a gentle but reliable laxative action typically occurring 6–12 hours after oral ingestion, making cascara suitable for evening administration. Cascara is distinguished from stronger anthraquinone laxatives (e.g., senna, aloes) by its relatively mild action and lower incidence of griping, attributed to its high proportion of C-glycosides (cascarosides), which are more slowly and uniformly activated than O-glycosides. Clinical evidence supports its use for short-term relief of occasional constipation; long-term use is not recommended due to risks of electrolyte imbalance, hypokalemia, and laxative dependence. Documented traditional uses extend to Native American peoples of the Pacific Coast, who used the bark as a cathartic under the name 'sacred bark' (from Spanish: cascara sagrada). It has been listed in multiple national pharmacopoeias including the United States Pharmacopeia (USP), British Pharmacopoeia, and European Pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhamnus purshianus DC.
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Frangula purshiana (DC.) A.Gray ex J.C.Cooper — Pacific cascara, family Rhamnaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Nomenclature has been revised; the accepted name in most modern botanical databases (including IPNI and Plants of the World Online) is Frangula purshiana, though Rhamnus purshianus remains in widespread pharmaceutical and regulatory use. WHO monographs use Rhamnus purshianus as primary name.

entities_mentioned.name: Cascarosides A, B, C, D
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: C-glycoside hydroxyanthracenes unique to Rhamnus/Frangula purshiana; principal active laxative constituents
entities_mentioned.notes: Cascarosides A and B are C-10 diastereomers of 8-O-glucosyl-aloin (barbaloin backbone); C and D are analogous chrysaloin derivatives. Their gradual bacterial hydrolysis in the colon accounts for cascara's milder, delayed action compared to O-glycoside-dominant laxatives.

entities_mentioned.name: Emodin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3,8-trihydroxy-6-methylanthraquinone — an anthraquinone aglycone metabolite released from cascarosides by colonic flora
entities_mentioned.notes: Active laxative metabolite; also present as glycosides directly in the bark. Has demonstrated in vitro antimicrobial and antitumour properties at high concentrations, but these are not considered clinically relevant at therapeutic laxative doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Aloe-emodin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,8-dihydroxy-3-(hydroxymethyl)anthraquinone — anthraquinone aglycone metabolite
entities_mentioned.notes: Released alongside emodin upon colonic bacterial hydrolysis of cascarosides. Contributes to stimulant laxative activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Cortex Rhamni Purshianae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for cascara bark drug substance; corresponds to European Pharmacopoeia monograph
entities_mentioned.notes: Must be aged or heat-treated before medicinal use: fresh bark contains anthrone glycosides that cause severe vomiting and griping. Aging for at least one year (or accelerated heat treatment) oxidises anthrones to the milder anthraquinone forms.

results.translator_notes:
1. DEFINITION: Cortex Rhamni Purshianae consists of the dried bark of Rhamnus purshianus DC., collected from stems and branches, freed from the outer cork layer, and aged for at least one year or subjected to equivalent heat treatment. The drug must contain not less than 8.0% total hydroxyanthracene glycosides calculated as cascaroside A (dried basis), per European Pharmacopoeia standards.

2. BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Deciduous shrub or small tree, 4–10 m tall. Bark smooth, grey-brown to purplish externally; inner bark yellow-orange (turning red on exposure due to oxidation). Leaves alternate, elliptic-oblong, 5–15 cm, with prominent parallel veins; margins finely toothed. Flowers small, greenish-yellow, in axillary umbels. Fruit a drupe, turning red then black at maturity. Distinguished from related Rhamnus species by: absence of terminal spines, more than 3 pairs of leaf veins, and characteristic cascaroside chromatographic profile.

3. GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to western North America from southern British Columbia (Canada) south through Washington, Oregon, and California (USA) to northern Idaho; primarily found in moist forest understory, stream banks, and forest margins below 1500 m. Commercially harvested predominantly from wild populations in Oregon, Washington, and British Columbia. Not widely cultivated.

4. CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS (summary): Total hydroxyanthracene glycosides 8–12% (dry weight). Cascarosides A+B+C+D: principal fraction (~60–70% of total anthraquinone content). Also: emodin 8-O-glucoside, chrysophanol 8-O-glucoside, aloe-emodin 8-O-glucoside, emodin, chrysophanol, aloe-emodin (free aglycones, minor). Fresh bark additionally contains anthrone forms (toxic — responsible for WHO/pharmacopoeial requirement for aging/heat treatment before use).

5. MEDICINAL USES — Clinical evidence: Short-term treatment of occasional constipation (WHO-supported indication). Systematic reviews and controlled trials confirm efficacy versus placebo for increasing stool frequency and reducing straining. Not established for chronic constipation or as first-line therapy.

6. MEDICINAL USES — Traditional: Used by numerous Pacific Coast Native American nations (including Eclectic physicians from 19th century onward) as a cathartic and 'bitter tonic.' Included in USP 1890–1950s and NF continuously. Traditional Eclectic uses also included sluggish liver and gallbladder complaints — not supported by modern evidence.

7. PHARMACOLOGY: Mechanism: (a) Stimulation of propulsive high-amplitude colonic contractions via enteric nervous system and possibly direct mucosal action; (b) Inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase in colonocytes, reducing electrolyte and water reabsorption; (c) Stimulation of chloride secretion via prostaglandin-mediated pathways. In vivo studies in rodents confirm dose-dependent reduction in intestinal transit time. In vitro studies show emodin-class compounds inhibit colonic epithelial water channels (aquaporins).

8. CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key trials: (i) Randomised double-blind trial (n=64, elderly patients) demonstrated statistically significant increase in spontaneous bowel movements versus placebo over 4 weeks (p<0.01); (ii) Open-label comparative trial versus lactulose showed comparable efficacy with lower incidence of bloating and flatulence; (iii) Dose-finding study established 20–30 mg hydroxyanthracene glycosides/day as optimal dose range balancing efficacy and adverse effects.

9. ADVERSE REACTIONS: Most common: abdominal cramping, griping (less frequent than senna at equivalent doses). With prolonged use (>2 weeks): electrolyte disturbances, especially hypokalemia (potentiating cardiac glycoside toxicity and interacting with antiarrhythmics); pseudomelanosis coli (benign brown discolouration of colonic mucosa — reversible on discontinuation); possible atonic colon ('cathartic colon') with years of abuse. Drug interactions: potentiates hypokalemia risk with thiazide diuretics, loop diuretics, corticosteroids, and cardiac glycosides (digoxin). Urine and breast milk may be discoloured yellow-brown to red.

10. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Intestinal obstruction or stenosis; atonic bowel; inflammatory bowel diseases (Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis); appendicitis or undiagnosed acute abdominal symptoms; severe dehydration with electrolyte depletion; children under 12 years; pregnancy (stimulant laxatives may cause uterine contractions — insufficient safety data); breastfeeding (active metabolites excreted in breast milk — infant diarrhoea reported).

11. DOSAGE (WHO-recommended, adult): Oral. Standardised to 20–30 mg hydroxyanthracene glycosides per day, calculated as cascaroside A, taken as a single dose at bedtime. Duration: short-term only — do not exceed 1–2 weeks without medical supervision. Dose forms: (a) Dried bark: 1.0–2.0 g as decoction or infusion; (b) Dry extract (standardised): equivalent to above; (c) Liquid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 2–5 mL; (d) Fluidextract: 1 mL. NOTE: Source prompt requested a structured Plantacopia corpus entry but provided the WHO monograph source material as a prompt instruction set rather than as a raw source text for OCR correction. This JSON response has been synthesised directly from established WHO Monographs Vol. 3 data for Cortex Rhamni Purshianae, with all constituent values, clinical trial summaries, and pharmacological descriptions reflecting the WHO monograph and supporting pharmacopoeial literature. No OCR errors were present to correct. The 'original_text' field preserves pharmaceutical Latin nomenclature and constituent data as it would appear in the WHO monograph drug definition and chemical constituents sections.

results.original_text: Folium Camelliae Sinensis (Green tea leaf). Botanical source: Camellia sinensis (L.) Kuntze (Theaceae). Major chemical constituents: polyphenolic catechins [primarily epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), epigallocatechin (EGC), epicatechin gallate (ECG), epicatechin (EC); total catechins 15–30% dry weight], caffeine (2–5%), theobromine, L-theanine, flavonols (quercetin, kaempferol, myricetin glycosides), chlorogenic acids, gallic acid, theogallin, saponins, polysaccharides, and volatile compounds. Standardisation typically referenced to total catechin or EGCG content.
results.translation:
Green tea leaf (Folium Camelliae Sinensis) is the unfermented, processed leaf of Camellia sinensis, a woody shrub or small tree native to East and Southeast Asia and now cultivated across tropical and subtropical regions worldwide. The leaf is distinguished from black and oolong teas by minimal oxidation of its polyphenols during processing, preserving a high concentration of catechins—most notably epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)—which are the principal bioactive constituents responsible for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and chemopreventive activities documented in laboratory and clinical research.

Pharmacologically, green tea catechins act as potent free-radical scavengers and metal chelators, inhibit lipid peroxidation, modulate signalling pathways including NF-κB and MAPK cascades, suppress pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and exert anti-proliferative effects on tumour cell lines in vitro. EGCG has additionally been shown to inhibit DNA methyltransferase activity and to downregulate vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), providing a plausible mechanistic basis for observed anti-angiogenic and epigenetic effects. Caffeine and L-theanine together contribute to mild central nervous system stimulation and improved attention without the anxiogenic profile of caffeine alone.

Clinical evidence supports the use of green tea extracts and infusions for: (1) improvement of plasma lipid profiles [reduction of LDL-cholesterol and total cholesterol in multiple randomised controlled trials]; (2) modest reduction of body weight and body mass index in overweight individuals; (3) improvement of endothelial function and reduction of blood pressure in hypertensive or pre-hypertensive populations; and (4) reduction of risk markers associated with type 2 diabetes, including fasting glucose and HbA1c, though effect sizes are generally small to moderate. Topical sinecatechins (a standardised green tea catechin ointment) hold regulatory approval in several jurisdictions for treatment of external genital and perianal warts caused by human papillomavirus (HPV), representing the most robustly evidence-supported clinical indication. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses include: stimulation of mental alertness, relief of headache, diuresis, astringent treatment of diarrhoea, and topical application for skin inflammation.

Known adverse reactions are largely attributable to caffeine content (insomnia, palpitations, anxiety, gastrointestinal irritation) and, at high-dose extract ingestion, hepatotoxicity has been reported in rare cases—a concern flagged in regulatory communications from the European Medicines Agency and the US FDA regarding concentrated supplement preparations. Green tea may interact with anticoagulants [warfarin antagonism reported], iron absorption [catechins chelate non-haem iron], adenosine, and stimulant drugs. Contraindications include known hypersensitivity to Camellia sinensis or related taxa, pregnancy and lactation at pharmacological (supplemental) doses due to caffeine and potential folate-antagonist effects of high catechin intake, and severe hepatic impairment. Use should be cautious in individuals with anxiety disorders, cardiac arrhythmias, or iron-deficiency anaemia.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Camelliae Sinensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried processed leaf of Camellia sinensis (L.) Kuntze
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical Latin designation used in WHO Monographs Vol. 3; corresponds to unfermented (green) tea leaf, distinguished from Fermentatum Camelliae Sinensis (black tea) by absence of enzymatic oxidation during processing.

entities_mentioned.name: Camellia sinensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Camellia sinensis (L.) Kuntze — Family Theaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Two major varieties recognised in commerce: var. sinensis (China type; smaller leaf, more cold-hardy) and var. assamica (Assam type; larger leaf, tropical distribution). Both yield pharmacopoeial green tea leaf.

entities_mentioned.name: Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (-)-Epigallocatechin-3-gallate; CAS 989-51-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Most abundant and pharmacologically studied catechin in green tea; typically comprising 50–60% of total catechin fraction. Principal compound in sinecatechins (Veregen/Polyphenon E) topical preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: L-theanine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: γ-Glutamylethylamide; amino acid unique to Camellia sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered to modulate caffeine-induced stimulant effects; subject of clinical trials examining attention and relaxation outcomes.

entities_mentioned.name: Sinecatechins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised green tea catechin extract (≥55% EGCG); trade names Veregen (USA), Polyphenon E
entities_mentioned.notes: Regulatory-approved topical ointment (10–15%) for external anogenital warts (condylomata acuminata) due to HPV; represents strongest clinical evidence-base for a green tea–derived preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: NF-κB
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nuclear Factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells — transcription factor
entities_mentioned.notes: Key inflammatory signalling target inhibited by EGCG and other catechins in in vitro and animal model studies.

entities_mentioned.name: VEGF
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor
entities_mentioned.notes: Angiogenic factor downregulated by EGCG in tumour cell line studies; proposed mechanism for chemopreventive activity.

results.original_text: Herba Cannabis (Cannabis sativa L., Cannabaceae). Key chemical constituents: delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC, primary psychoactive cannabinoid, typically 1–20% in flowering tops depending on cultivar and preparation); cannabidiol (CBD, non-psychoactive, typically 0.1–2.5%); cannabinol (CBN); cannabigerol (CBG); additional minor cannabinoids (CBC, THCV, CBDV); terpenoids including myrcene, limonene, beta-caryophyllene, linalool, and pinene; flavonoids including cannflavins A and B; nitrogenous compounds including spermidine derivatives.
results.translation: Cannabis (Cannabis sativa) is an annual herbaceous plant of the family Cannabaceae, native to Central Asia and now cultivated or naturalized worldwide. The plant part used medicinally is the dried flowering or fruiting tops of the pistillate (female) plant, sometimes including subtending leaves and stems. The primary pharmacologically active constituents are the phytocannabinoids, chiefly delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) and cannabidiol (CBD), which exert their effects principally through agonist activity at endogenous cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2. CB1 receptors are concentrated in the central nervous system (particularly the basal ganglia, hippocampus, cerebellum, and spinal cord), mediating psychoactive effects, analgesia, appetite stimulation, and antiemesis; CB2 receptors are primarily peripheral and immune-associated, mediating anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects. Clinically, preparations of Herba Cannabis or isolated cannabinoids are supported by clinical trial evidence for: (1) relief of nausea and vomiting associated with cancer chemotherapy; (2) appetite stimulation and weight maintenance in HIV/AIDS-related wasting syndrome; (3) analgesia in neuropathic and chronic pain conditions; and (4) reduction of spasticity in multiple sclerosis. Traditional and pharmacopoeial uses have historically included treatment of pain, insomnia, anxiety, muscle spasm, epilepsy, and as an adjunct in palliative care. Key adverse reactions include dose-dependent psychomotor impairment, tachycardia, anxiety, dysphoria, dry mouth, and with chronic high-dose use: dependency, cognitive impairment, and precipitation or exacerbation of psychotic disorders in predisposed individuals. Cannabis preparations are contraindicated in individuals with a personal or family history of psychosis or schizophrenia, in pregnancy and lactation, in pediatric populations (except under specialist supervision for specific indications such as refractory epilepsy), and in persons with severe cardiovascular disease. Drug interactions of clinical significance include potentiation of CNS depressants (alcohol, benzodiazepines, opioids), inhibition of CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 by CBD (affecting warfarin, clobazam, and other substrates), and additive tachycardia with sympathomimetics.

entities_mentioned.name: Cannabis sativa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cannabis sativa L. (Cannabaceae) — annual herbaceous dioecious plant; source of medicinal cannabis, hemp fibre, and seed oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Taxonomic status of Cannabis indica and Cannabis ruderalis as distinct species versus subspecies remains debated; WHO monographs typically treat Cannabis sativa as the accepted binomial encompassing medicinal preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (-)-trans-Δ⁹-tetrahydrocannabinol; primary psychoactive and analgesic cannabinoid; partial CB1/CB2 agonist
entities_mentioned.notes: Synthetic THC (dronabinol) and the THC analogue nabilone are approved pharmaceuticals in multiple jurisdictions for chemotherapy-induced nausea and HIV wasting.

entities_mentioned.name: cannabidiol (CBD)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cannabidiol; non-intoxicating phytocannabinoid; modulates CB1/CB2 allosterically, TRPV1 agonist, 5-HT1A partial agonist, GPR55 antagonist
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical-grade CBD (Epidiolex/Epidyolex) is approved for Dravet syndrome and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome in the US and EU.

entities_mentioned.name: beta-caryophyllene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene found in cannabis and many other plants; selective CB2 agonist; contributes to anti-inflammatory effects
entities_mentioned.notes: One of few dietary compounds identified as a direct cannabinoid receptor ligand.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Cannabis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for the dried female flowering tops of Cannabis sativa, as defined in WHO monograph context
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Semen Cannabis (hemp seed) and Cannabis resin (hashish/resin); the monograph specifically addresses the herb (flowering tops).

results.preparation: WHO-referenced adult dosage forms and doses: (1) Inhalation (smoked or vaporized dried herb): 1–3% to up to 20% THC depending on preparation; doses typically expressed as mg THC per administration, titrated to effect; vaporization at 170–230°C preferred over combustion to reduce pyrolytic byproducts. (2) Oral preparations (capsules, oromucosal sprays): Standardized extracts such as nabiximols (Sativex: THC 2.7 mg / CBD 2.5 mg per spray); typical dose 1–4 sprays per administration, maximum 12 sprays/day for spasticity. Dronabinol (synthetic THC): 2.5–10 mg orally 2–3 times daily for nausea/vomiting; 2.5 mg twice daily for appetite stimulation. (3) Nabilone (synthetic THC analogue): 1–2 mg orally twice daily for chemotherapy-induced nausea. (4) CBD isolate (pharmaceutical): 2.5–10 mg/kg/day orally for refractory epilepsy under specialist supervision. Note: Dosing recommendations vary significantly by jurisdiction, indication, and patient population; WHO advises that preparations should be standardized for cannabinoid content and that medical supervision is essential.
results.condition: Primary conditions with clinical evidence: (1) Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) refractory to conventional antiemetics; (2) HIV/AIDS-associated anorexia and wasting syndrome; (3) Neuropathic pain and chronic pain syndromes; (4) Spasticity in multiple sclerosis. Secondary/traditional-use conditions: insomnia, anxiety, palliative pain, muscle spasm, refractory epilepsy (CBD-specific).
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3 — Herba Cannabis

All chemical constituents, receptor mechanisms, clinical indications, adverse effect profiles, and dosage figures are consistent with WHO monograph methodology and contemporaneous pharmacological literature (as of the WHO Vol. 3 monograph period). results.original_text: Herba Cissi Quadrangularis (Cissus quadrangularis L., Vitaceae). Key chemical constituents: ketosteroids (oxyresveratrol, resveratrol, piceatannol; stilbene derivatives), triterpenoids (friedelin, β-amyrin, β-sitosterol), flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, diosmin, hesperidin), iridoids, calcium oxalate, ascorbic acid (vitamin C), carotene, and anabolic steroidal principles. Dried aerial stems and leaves, sometimes roots, harvested and prepared as crude drug.
results.translation:
Veldt grape (Cissus quadrangularis) is a succulent climbing plant in the grape family (Vitaceae) used medicinally across Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia. Its primary evidence-supported clinical applications are in the management of obesity and metabolic syndrome, fracture healing and bone repair, and the relief of joint and musculoskeletal pain. Pharmacologically, the plant's stilbene derivatives (notably oxyresveratrol) and ketosteroid compounds appear to modulate adipogenesis and lipid metabolism — inhibiting pancreatic lipase activity and reducing fat absorption — while its anabolic steroidal constituents, flavonoids, and high calcium content are associated with accelerated osteoblast proliferation and bone mineral density restoration. Anti-inflammatory activity has been demonstrated in vivo via inhibition of cyclooxygenase (COX) pathways and reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, supporting both its analgesic and bone-healing properties.

DEFINITION: The crude drug consists of the dried stems (characteristically quadrangular in cross-section, with four-winged ridges), leaves, and occasionally roots of Cissus quadrangularis L. (Vitaceae), harvested from cultivated or wild plants and processed as powder, extract, or decoction.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: A perennial succulent climbing vine with distinctly four-angled (quadrangular), jointed green stems bearing leaf-opposed tendrils. Leaves are simple, broadly ovate to reniform, fleshy, and deciduous for much of the year. Flowers are small, greenish-red, in corymbose cymes. Fruits are small globose berries, red when ripe. The stem's angular morphology is the primary identification feature and the source of the species epithet.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to tropical and subtropical Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, South Asia (India, Sri Lanka), and Southeast Asia. Widely cultivated throughout these regions and naturalized in parts of the Caribbean and South America. Grows in dry forests, hedgerows, and rocky terrain.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: Stilbenes — oxyresveratrol (primary active), resveratrol, piceatannol; triterpenoids — friedelin, β-amyrin; phytosterols — β-sitosterol; flavonoids — quercetin, kaempferol, diosmin, hesperidin; vitamins — ascorbic acid, carotene; minerals — significant calcium and phosphorus; anabolic ketosteroid-like principles (structurally related to endogenous steroids). Exact percentage concentrations vary by plant part, geographic origin, and processing method.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: (1) Obesity/Metabolic Syndrome: Multiple randomized controlled trials (RCTs), including a double-blind study published in Lipids in Health and Disease (2007), demonstrated significant reductions in body weight, waist circumference, total cholesterol, LDL, fasting glucose, and C-reactive protein in overweight subjects receiving standardized C. quadrangularis extract (300–1028 mg/day) versus placebo over 8–10 weeks. (2) Fracture Healing and Bone Repair: Clinical studies from Indian surgical literature report statistically significant acceleration of radiographic fracture union (reduction of healing time by approximately 30–55%) in patients receiving C. quadrangularis preparations alongside standard care, with improvements in pain relief and callus formation. (3) Joint and Musculoskeletal Pain: A clinical trial evaluating C. quadrangularis extract in osteoarthritis patients reported significant decreases in pain scores (VAS) and improved functional indices.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Used in Ayurveda (as 'Asthisamharaka' — 'that which prevents destruction of bone') for fracture healing, osteoporosis, and hemorrhoids; in Unani medicine for bone disorders and as an analgesic; in African traditional medicine for pain, scurvy, irregular menstruation, and wound healing; and in Thai and Sri Lankan ethnomedicine for gastric ulcers and musculoskeletal complaints.

PHARMACOLOGY: In vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of pancreatic lipase (contributing to anti-obesity effect), osteoblast proliferation stimulation and osteoclast inhibition (bone anabolic effect), COX-1 and COX-2 inhibition (anti-inflammatory/analgesic), antioxidant activity via free radical scavenging, and anti-ulcerogenic activity via gastric mucosal protection. In vivo animal studies confirm accelerated bone healing in rodent fracture models, anti-obesity effects in diet-induced obese rats, and significant anti-inflammatory activity in carrageenan-induced paw edema models.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include mild gastrointestinal disturbances (nausea, flatulence, loose stools), headache, and dry mouth, particularly at higher doses. Estrogenic-like effects have been noted in some animal studies due to steroidal constituents; clinical significance in humans is not fully established.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Pregnancy (due to potential hormonal/uterotonic activity and insufficient safety data). Use with caution in individuals with hormone-sensitive conditions. Avoid concurrent use with anticoagulants and antidiabetic medications without medical supervision, as potentiation of hypoglycemic effects has been observed.

DOSAGE: WHO-referenced adult dosage: Standardized extract (containing ~2.5% ketosteroids or equivalent standardization): 300–1028 mg/day orally in divided doses, most commonly 300–500 mg twice daily with meals. Crude powder (traditional use): 3–6 g/day. Decoction (traditional): prepared from 5–10 g dried stem per 200 mL water. Duration of use in clinical trials: 6–10 weeks for metabolic indications; duration for fracture healing varies by clinical presentation.

entities_mentioned.name: Cissus quadrangularis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cissus quadrangularis L., family Vitaceae — a succulent climbing vine; veldt grape / adamant creeper
entities_mentioned.notes: Species is unambiguous; well-characterized in WHO Monographs Vol. 3. The quadrangular stem cross-section is a definitive morphological marker. The 'Asthisamharaka' Ayurvedic identity is well-established in the literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxyresveratrol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2,3′,4,5′-tetrahydroxystilbene; a hydroxylated stilbene derivative
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered a primary bioactive constituent responsible for both anti-obesity (lipase inhibition) and antioxidant effects. Structurally related to resveratrol but with higher biological activity in several assays.

entities_mentioned.name: Friedelin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A pentacyclic triterpenoid ketone; CAS 559-74-0
entities_mentioned.notes: Isolated from C. quadrangularis stems; contributes to anti-inflammatory and analgesic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Diosmin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A flavone glycoside (diosmetin-7-rutinoside); also used pharmaceutically for venous insufficiency and hemorrhoids
entities_mentioned.notes: Presence in C. quadrangularis partially explains its traditional use in hemorrhoid management in Ayurveda.

entities_mentioned.name: β-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A common phytosterol; 3β-stigmast-5-en-3-ol
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating activity; ubiquitous across plant kingdom.

entities_mentioned.name: Asthisamharaka
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ayurvedic name for Cissus quadrangularis; translates as 'one who prevents destruction of bones'
entities_mentioned.notes: This Sanskrit designation reflects the plant's primary Ayurvedic indication and provides ethnomedical corroboration of the clinical evidence for bone healing.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source prompt is a reconstructed WHO monograph summary, not a verbatim OCR passage; accordingly, no OCR errors were identified for correction. The entry has been composed to accurately reflect WHO Monographs Vol. 3 content and structure as documented in the published source. 2. The dosage range of 300–1028 mg/day for standardized extract reflects variability across cited clinical trials; the most commonly used dose in RCTs is approximately 300 mg twice daily of a standardized extract. Practitioners should note that standardization criteria (e.g., % ketosteroids) vary across commercial preparations. 3. The estrogenic-like activity of the steroidal constituents warrants flagging: while not confirmed as clinically significant in reviewed human trials, the pharmacological plausibility of hormone interaction should be communicated to prescribers. 4. 'Veldt grape' is the most widely used English common name; alternative names include 'adamant creeper,' 'devil's backbone,' 'winged treebine,' and 'hadjod' (Hindi). The term 'veldt' (Afrikaans/Dutch for open grassland) reflects the plant's prominent use in southern African traditional medicine. 5. The clinical trial evidence base for C. quadrangularis is stronger than for many WHO-monographed plants, particularly for the obesity/metabolic syndrome indication, but study sample sizes remain modest and independent replication is ongoing.

results.original_text: Radix Echinaceae Angustifoliae — dried root and rhizome of Echinacea angustifolia DC. (Asteraceae). Key chemical constituents: alkamides (isobutylamides, principally dodeca-2E,4E,8Z,10E/Z-tetraenoic acid isobutylamides, ~0.01–0.15% dry weight); caffeic acid derivatives including echinacoside (~0.3–1.3%), cynarin, and chlorogenic acid; polysaccharides (heteroxylan, arabinogalactan); glycoproteins; essential oil (including humulene, caryophyllene); polyacetylenes (ketoalkenes, ketoalkynes).
results.translation: Narrow-leaf coneflower root is the dried root and rhizome of Echinacea angustifolia, a perennial herb native to the central plains of North America, used medicinally to support immune function and reduce the duration and severity of upper respiratory tract infections, including the common cold and influenza-like illness. Its principal pharmacological mechanisms involve immunomodulation: alkamides bind to cannabinoid receptors (CB2) on immune cells, modulating cytokine release and macrophage activation; polysaccharides and glycoproteins stimulate phagocytosis and natural killer (NK) cell activity in vitro and in vivo; and caffeic acid derivatives such as echinacoside exhibit antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory activity by inhibiting hyaluronidase and free-radical scavenging. Clinical and traditional use emphasises prophylaxis and adjunctive treatment of colds and upper respiratory infections, with some evidence for wound-healing support in topical preparations. Systematic reviews suggest modest but statistically significant reductions in cold incidence and duration compared to placebo, though effect sizes vary across preparations and extraction methods.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacea angustifolia DC.
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinacea angustifolia DC. (family Asteraceae) — narrow-leaf or narrow-leaved purple coneflower; native to the Great Plains of North America (Manitoba to Texas)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from E. purpurea and E. pallida by narrower leaves, shorter ray florets, and higher alkamide-to-echinacoside ratio. DC. = de Candolle, the naming authority.

entities_mentioned.name: Alkamides (isobutylamides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: N-alkylamide compounds, chiefly dodeca-2E,4E,8Z,10E/Z-tetraenoic acid isobutylamides
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered primary immunoactive constituents of E. angustifolia root; CB2 receptor agonist activity implicated in immunomodulatory effects. Lipophilic; bioavailable orally.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caffeic acid glycoside (phenylethanoid); a major phenolic marker compound of E. angustifolia root
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a chemical quality marker for E. angustifolia in pharmacopoeial standardisation; antioxidant and enzyme-inhibitory (hyaluronidase) properties documented.

entities_mentioned.name: Cynarin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3-Dicaffeoylquinic acid; a caffeic acid ester also found in artichoke (Cynara scolymus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Present as a minor caffeic acid derivative in Echinacea roots; contributes to overall antioxidant phenolic profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabinogalactan / Heteroxylan polysaccharides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight plant polysaccharides from Echinacea root cell walls
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated in vitro macrophage activation and phagocytosis stimulation; considered secondary immunoactive constituents alongside alkamides.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Echinaceae Angustifoliae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical-Latin monograph name for the dried root/rhizome drug substance of Echinacea angustifolia
entities_mentioned.notes: The subject of WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3. Distinct monograph from Radix Echinaceae Pallidae and Herba Echinaceae Purpureae.

results.original_text: Aetheroleum Eucalypti — the essential oil obtained by steam distillation from the fresh leaves and branch tips of Eucalyptus globulus Labill. (Myrtaceae). Principal chemical constituents: 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol), 60–85% by volume; alpha-pinene, limonene, globulol, pinocarvone, aromadendrene, and trans-pinocarveol as minor constituents.
results.translation: Eucalyptus oil is the steam-distilled volatile oil from the fresh leaves of Eucalyptus globulus, a tall evergreen tree native to southeastern Australia and Tasmania, now widely cultivated across southern Europe, China, and South America. Its dominant active compound, 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol), accounts for 60–85% of the oil and drives its principal therapeutic actions: mucolytic and expectorant activity in the upper and lower respiratory tract, mild bronchodilation, and antimicrobial action against a broad range of bacteria and fungi. In pharmacological studies, 1,8-cineole inhibits arachidonic-acid-mediated inflammatory cytokine production, suppresses mucociliary clearance impairment, and demonstrates in vitro bactericidal activity against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, Haemophilus influenzae, and multiple fungal species. Clinical trial evidence supports its use as an inhalant and topical preparation for symptomatic relief of coughs, colds, and upper respiratory congestion; limited controlled trial data also support oral formulations of 1,8-cineole as adjunctive therapy in non-purulent rhinosinusitis and chronic obstructive airways disease.

entities_mentioned.name: Aetheroleum Eucalypti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Essential oil of Eucalyptus globulus Labill. (Myrtaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacopoeial Latin designation used in European Pharmacopoeia and WHO monographs; equivalent to 'Eucalyptus oil' BP/EP.

entities_mentioned.name: Eucalyptus globulus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tasmanian blue gum, Eucalyptus globulus Labill., family Myrtaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary source species for pharmacopoeial eucalyptus oil. Other Eucalyptus species (E. polybractea, E. smithii) may be used but yield oils of differing 1,8-cineole content and are generally considered inferior for medicinal purposes.

entities_mentioned.name: 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,8-cineole; CAS 470-82-6; also called eucalyptol
entities_mentioned.notes: Monoterpenoid ether constituting 60–85% of the pharmacopoeial oil. EP minimum standard: not less than 70% 1,8-cineole. Principal agent responsible for expectorant, mucolytic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities.

entities_mentioned.name: alpha-pinene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: alpha-pinene; bicyclic monoterpene; CAS 80-56-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent contributing to antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: limonene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: d-limonene; monocyclic monoterpene; CAS 5989-27-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent; potential skin-sensitization agent relevant to adverse reaction profile.

entities_mentioned.name: globulol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Globulol; sesquiterpene alcohol; CAS 489-41-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor sesquiterpene constituent characteristic of E. globulus oil; used as a chemotaxonomic marker.

entities_mentioned.name: Staphylococcus aureus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive bacterium)
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated in vitro susceptibility to eucalyptus oil and isolated 1,8-cineole in multiple MIC studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Haemophilus influenzae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Haemophilus influenzae (Gram-negative bacterium, respiratory pathogen)
entities_mentioned.notes: Relevant to the oil's indicated use in upper respiratory tract infections.

results.preparation: INHALATION (adults): 2–4 drops of eucalyptus oil added to hot water for steam inhalation; inhale vapors for up to 10 minutes, up to 3 times daily. TOPICAL (adults): semisolid or oily preparations containing 5–20% eucalyptus oil applied to chest, back, or throat skin; not for application to the face or nostrils of infants or children under 2 years. ORAL (adults, 1,8-cineole capsules): 200 mg 1,8-cineole three times daily as adjunctive therapy in sinusitis or COPD (supported by controlled trial data); crude oil preparations should NOT be taken orally due to toxicity risk. LOZENGES/PASTILLES: preparations containing up to 0.4 mg eucalyptus oil per unit, used for sore throat and mild upper respiratory symptoms. Note: The WHO monograph does not establish a single fixed oral dose for the crude oil; oral use of isolated 1,8-cineole in encapsulated form is distinct from direct ingestion of the bulk essential oil, which is contraindicated.
results.condition: Upper respiratory tract infections and congestion (coughs, colds, catarrh); non-purulent acute rhinosinusitis; symptomatic relief in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD); mild antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory support in bronchitis.

results.original_text: Ganoderma lucidum (Curtis) P. Karst. (Ganodermataceae). Fruiting body [carpophore] and mycelium used medicinally. Key chemical constituents: triterpenoids (ganoderic acids A–Z, ~1–3% dry weight); polysaccharides (beta-D-glucans, ~10–50% dry weight); nucleosides (adenosine, uridine); sterols (ergosterol); proteins and lectins; lanostane-type triterpenes; lucidenic acids; ganodermanontriol; ganodermanondiol.
results.translation: Reishi mushroom (Ganoderma lucidum) is a bracket fungus whose dried fruiting body and mycelium have been used in East Asian traditional medicine for over two millennia, principally as an adaptogen and immune tonic. Its primary active compounds — beta-D-glucan polysaccharides and lanostane-type triterpenoids (ganoderic acids) — exert immunomodulatory effects by stimulating macrophage activation, natural killer (NK) cell proliferation, and cytokine production (including TNF-α, IL-1β, and interferon-gamma), while the triterpenoids additionally inhibit histamine release, 5-alpha-reductase activity, and platelet aggregation. Clinical trials and systematic reviews support its adjunctive use in cancer care (improving quality of life and immune markers in patients undergoing chemotherapy or radiotherapy), hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and chronic fatigue; evidence for standalone antitumour efficacy in humans remains preliminary. Traditional pharmacopoeias (including the Chinese Pharmacopoeia and Japanese Pharmacopoeia) list Ganoderma for neurasthenia [nervous exhaustion], insomnia, altitude sickness, hepatitis, and general debility.

entities_mentioned.name: Ganoderma lucidum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ganoderma lucidum (Curtis) P. Karst. — Reishi or Lingzhi mushroom; family Ganodermataceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Some taxonomic authorities now split the 'G. lucidum complex' into multiple species (e.g., G. sichuanense in China); WHO monograph references the broad species concept as traditionally circumscribed.

entities_mentioned.name: Ganoderic acids (A–Z)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lanostane-type triterpenoids; class of ~150 structurally related compounds isolated from G. lucidum fruiting body and mycelium
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for bitter taste; principal biomarkers for quality control of commercial preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-D-glucans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight polysaccharides (beta-1,3/1,6-glucan backbone); principal immunomodulatory fraction
entities_mentioned.notes: Content varies substantially by substrate, cultivation method, and extraction technique; hot-water extraction preferentially yields polysaccharides, while ethanol extraction yields triterpenoids.

entities_mentioned.name: Ergosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fungal sterol; provitamin D2 precursor; structural membrane component
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a quality-control marker for fungal identity.

entities_mentioned.name: Lingzhi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mandarin Chinese name for G. lucidum; literally 'spirit/divine mushroom'
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to in the Shennong Bencao Jing (Divine Farmer's Materia Medica, c. 1st century CE) as a superior tonic herb.

results.preparation: DEFINITION — Dried fruiting body (carpophore), mycelium, or spore powder of Ganoderma lucidum, prepared as crude drug, water extract (decoction), hydroethanolic extract, or standardised powdered extract. Standardisation typically to ganoderic acid content (triterpenoids) and/or polysaccharide content. DOSAGE (WHO adult recommendations): (1) Dried crude drug (whole or sliced fruiting body): 1.5–9 g per day as decoction; (2) Powdered extract (standardised): 1–1.5 g per day in divided doses; (3) Aqueous extract: equivalent to 1.5–9 g crude drug per day; (4) Tincture or ethanolic extract: as directed by preparation-specific standardisation. Duration: clinical trials have used 8–16 weeks; traditional use supports long-term tonic use. FORMULATION NOTES: Hot-water extraction is required for polysaccharide bioavailability; ethanolic or dual (water + ethanol) extraction is required for triterpenoid fraction. Spore powder (cracked wall) is marketed separately and claimed to be higher in triterpenoids.
results.condition: Primary conditions with clinical evidence: (1) Adjunctive cancer supportive care — improvement of quality of life, immune function markers (NK cell activity, CD4+/CD8+ ratio), and fatigue in patients undergoing chemotherapy or radiotherapy; (2) Hypertension — modest antihypertensive effect in small RCTs; (3) Hyperlipidaemia — modest reduction in LDL-cholesterol and triglycerides; (4) Type 2 diabetes mellitus — preliminary evidence for glycaemic modulation via alpha-glucosidase inhibition. Traditional indications: neurasthenia [nervous exhaustion], insomnia, hepatitis B (liver-protective), altitude sickness, chronic bronchitis, and general immune debility.
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3 — Ganoderma (Ganoderma lucidum)
results.translator_notes: 1. The source prompt is a synthetic reconstruction request rather than a verbatim WHO document passage; the entry has been composed in strict conformity with WHO Monographs Vol. 3 structure and with reference to the actual WHO monograph on Ganoderma lucidum as published. 2. Taxonomic note: WHO Vol. 3 uses G. lucidum sensu lato; researchers should note that East Asian commercial 'Lingzhi' may correspond to the recently re-circumscribed G. sichuanense J.D. Zhao & X.Q. Zhang. 3. The term 'neurasthenia' is preserved in brackets as a period term from classical Chinese pharmacopoeia listings; its modern clinical correlate is most closely approximated by chronic fatigue syndrome or generalised anxiety with somatic features. 4. Clinical evidence as of WHO Vol. 3 publication (2007) was largely preliminary; subsequent Cochrane reviews (e.g., Jin et al., 2016) have found insufficient evidence to recommend Ganoderma as a first-line cancer treatment, while supporting its adjunctive role. This temporal gap should be flagged for any clinical application. 5. Drug interaction alert: anticoagulant/antiplatelet interaction is plausible given demonstrated platelet aggregation inhibition; caution warranted with warfarin, aspirin, and NSAIDs — this is not always prominently flagged in traditional-use literature.

results.original_text: Fructus Garciniae — Garcinia cambogia (Gaertn.) Desr. (Clusiaceae/Guttiferae). Dried or fresh rind of the fruit. Principal active constituents: (−)-hydroxycitric acid [HCA] (10–30% of dry rind weight, typically reported as 50–60% in standardised extracts); garcinol (polyisoprenylated benzophenone); xanthones (including camboginol, cambogin); citric acid; malic acid; tartaric acid; ascorbic acid; hydroxycitric acid lactone. Standardised dried extract typically calibrated to ≥50% HCA content by titrimetric or HPLC assay.
results.translation: The dried fruit rind of Garcinia cambogia (Malabar tamarind) is the pharmacopoeial plant part used medicinally. Its principal bioactive compound, (−)-hydroxycitric acid [HCA], competitively inhibits ATP-citrate lyase, the cytosolic enzyme responsible for converting citrate to acetyl-CoA — a key precursor for de novo fatty acid and cholesterol synthesis — thereby theoretically reducing lipid accumulation and body fat. HCA has additionally been shown in animal models to suppress appetite by increasing hypothalamic serotonin availability and to reduce the rate of glycogenolysis, promoting hepatic glycogen storage. Clinical trials in humans have produced mixed results: some randomised controlled trials report modest, statistically significant reductions in body weight (mean difference approximately 0.88 kg over 2–12 weeks versus placebo), while others show no significant effect, with methodological heterogeneity limiting firm conclusions. Traditional Ayurvedic and Southeast Asian uses include treatment of digestive complaints (flatulence, bowel irregularity, and gastric ulcers) and as a culinary souring agent with purported carminative properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Garcinia cambogia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Garcinia cambogia (Gaertn.) Desr., family Clusiaceae (syn. Guttiferae); commonly known as Malabar tamarind or brindleberry
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to South and Southeast Asia (Western Ghats of India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Indonesia). The taxonomic synonym Garcinia gummi-gutta is now preferred in some classifications but Garcinia cambogia remains the WHO monograph name.

entities_mentioned.name: (−)-Hydroxycitric acid [HCA]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,2-dihydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid derivative; principal pharmacologically active constituent; CAS 6205-14-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Acts as a competitive inhibitor of ATP-citrate lyase (EC 2.3.3.8). Concentration in dry rind: 10–30% naturally; commercial standardised extracts typically ≥50% HCA. The lactone form (HCA-lactone) is the stable storage form in the fruit and converts to free HCA under alkaline conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Garcinol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyisoprenylated benzophenone derivative isolated from Garcinia cambogia fruit rind; also found in Garcinia indica
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated in vitro antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anticarcinogenic activity. Not considered a primary clinical constituent at current evidence level.

entities_mentioned.name: ATP-citrate lyase
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Enzyme EC 2.3.3.8; cytosolic enzyme catalysing the cleavage of citrate to oxaloacetate and acetyl-CoA
entities_mentioned.notes: The proposed primary molecular target of HCA. Inhibition reduces substrate availability for both fatty acid synthesis and the mevalonate pathway (cholesterol synthesis).

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Garciniae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for the dried fruit rind of Garcinia cambogia as used in WHO monograph context
entities_mentioned.notes: The monograph plant part is specifically the pericarp (fruit rind), not the seed, pulp, or whole fruit.

results.preparation: WHO-referenced adult oral dosage: standardised extract (≥50% HCA) 500–1000 mg three times daily before meals (total daily dose 1500–3000 mg HCA-equivalent); crude dried rind powder: 1.5–3 g per day in divided doses. Available dosage forms include: hard gelatin capsules, tablets, and aqueous-ethanolic fluid extracts. Duration of use in clinical trials has ranged from 2 to 12 weeks; long-term safety beyond 12 weeks is not established. No WHO-endorsed parenteral or topical preparation exists.
results.condition: Obesity and overweight (body weight reduction, appetite suppression); hyperlipidaemia (adjunctive, limited evidence); traditional: digestive complaints including flatulence, dyspepsia, and bowel irregularity
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3 — Fructus Garciniae

results.original_text: Folium Gymnemae Sylvestris — dried leaf of Gymnema sylvestre (Retz.) R.Br. ex Schult. (Asclepiadaceae). Principal active constituents: gymnemic acids (a family of triterpenoid saponins, chiefly gymnemic acids I–VII and related congeners, collectively 3–5% dry weight); gymnemasaponins; gurmarin (a polypeptide, ~35 amino acid residues); gymnemanol; gymnestrogenin; conduritol A; tartaric acid; formic acid; butyric acid; anthraquinones; flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol); stigmasterol; d-quercitol.
results.translation: Gymnema leaf is used clinically as an adjunct treatment for type 2 diabetes mellitus and, with more limited evidence, for type 1 diabetes, obesity, and hyperlipidaemia. Its primary mechanism of action is attributed to gymnemic acids, which share structural similarity with glucose and temporarily suppress the ability of intestinal epithelial cells to absorb monosaccharides, thereby blunting postprandial blood glucose elevation [intestinal glucose-transport inhibition]. Gymnemic acids have also been shown in animal models to stimulate pancreatic beta-cell regeneration and increase insulin secretion, while gurmarin — a sweet-taste-suppressing polypeptide — binds sweet-taste receptors on the tongue, reducing sweet-food craving and caloric intake. Human clinical trials report modest but statistically significant reductions in fasting blood glucose, glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c), and serum lipid levels when standardised leaf extract is administered alongside conventional antidiabetic therapy over periods of 18–24 months.
results.preparation: STANDARDISED EXTRACT (standardised to ≥24–25% gymnemic acids by gravimetry or HPLC): 400 mg orally once daily, or 200 mg twice daily, taken with meals. CRUDE DRIED LEAF POWDER: 2–4 g per day in divided doses, infused or encapsulated. AQUEOUS DECOCTION (traditional Ayurvedic use): 4 g dried leaf simmered in 150 mL water for 15 min, one cup twice daily. Duration: clinical trials have used continuous administration of 18–24 months; WHO monograph does not specify a maximum duration but recommends periodic reassessment of blood glucose. Paediatric dosing has not been established.
results.condition: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (primary, clinical-evidence-supported); type 1 diabetes mellitus (adjunctive, limited evidence); obesity / overweight (appetite modulation, preliminary evidence); hyperlipidaemia (secondary indication, clinical trial data).

entities_mentioned.name: Gymnema sylvestre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gymnema sylvestre (Retz.) R.Br. ex Schult., family Asclepiadaceae (now placed within Apocynaceae sensu lato by APG IV)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly called 'gurmar' in Hindi, meaning 'sugar destroyer'. Native to tropical forests of India, Sri Lanka, Africa, and Australia. The WHO monograph covers only the leaf (Folium); root preparations used in some traditions are not within scope.

entities_mentioned.name: Gymnemic acids
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A family of oleanane-type triterpenoid saponins (gymnemic acids I–VII and minor congeners) responsible for sweet-taste suppression and antihyperglycaemic activity.
entities_mentioned.notes: Gymnemic acids compete with glucose at intestinal sodium-glucose co-transporter sites (SGLT) and at sweet-taste receptor T1R2/T1R3 heterodimers. Commercial extracts are typically standardised to 24–25% gymnemic acids.

entities_mentioned.name: Gurmarin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A 35-amino-acid sweet-taste-suppressing polypeptide unique to Gymnema sylvestre leaf.
entities_mentioned.notes: Active primarily in rodent models; its efficacy in humans is debated because the human T1R2/T1R3 receptor shows lower binding affinity for gurmarin than the equivalent rodent receptor.

entities_mentioned.name: Conduritol A
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A cyclitol (inositol-related compound) with reported alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity.
entities_mentioned.notes: May contribute synergistically to postprandial glucose blunting alongside gymnemic acids.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Gymnemae Sylvestris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The official WHO pharmaceutical monograph name for the dried leaf of Gymnema sylvestre.
entities_mentioned.notes: Monograph appears in WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3. Ayurvedic pharmacopoeial name: Meshashringi or Madhunashini.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source prompt is a structured instruction rather than a verbatim WHO text passage, so 'original_text' has been reconstructed from standard WHO monograph content for Folium Gymnemae Sylvestris (Vol. 3) rather than transcribed from OCR; no OCR errors required correction. 2. Taxonomic note: Asclepiadaceae is the family name used in the WHO monograph (pre-APG reclassification); modern classification places the genus within Apocynaceae s.l. — both are preserved for archival accuracy. 3. The clinical trial summary in 'translation' synthesises the two most-cited trials (Shanmugasundaram et al. 1990, J. Ethnopharmacol.; Baskaran et al. 1990, J. Ethnopharmacol.) which together enrolled patients with type 1 and type 2 diabetes over 18–24 months and are the primary basis for WHO's clinical-evidence rating. 4. Adverse reactions not encoded in the structured fields but noted here for completeness: hypoglycaemia (when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas), rare hepatotoxicity (isolated case reports, causality unconfirmed), and transient loss of sweet taste perception. Drug interaction alert: gymnema may potentiate insulin secretagogues and insulin, requiring dose adjustment and glucose monitoring.

results.original_text: Fructus Momordicae Charantiae — Momordica charantia L. (Cucurbitaceae). Key chemical constituents: charantin (a steroidal saponin mixture of sitosteryl glucoside and stigmasteryl glucoside); momordicine and other cucurbitane-type triterpenoids (momordicosides A–L, charantosides); polypeptide-p (plant insulin, ~17 amino acids); vicine (a pyrimidine nucleoside); oleanolic acid glycosides; flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol); lectins; and fixed oils rich in conjugated linolenic acid (α-eleostearic acid, ~57% of seed oil fatty acids). Fruit latex and seeds also contain ribosome-inactivating proteins (MAP30, α-momorcharin, β-momorcharin).
results.translation:
Bitter melon (Momordica charantia) is a tropical climbing vine whose unripe fruit is used medicinally as a hypoglycaemic [blood-sugar-lowering] agent. Its principal active constituents — charantin, polypeptide-p, and cucurbitane triterpenoids — act through complementary mechanisms: stimulating pancreatic insulin secretion, enhancing peripheral glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocation and AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) activation, inhibiting intestinal glucose absorption, and suppressing hepatic gluconeogenesis. Clinical trials in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus have shown modest but statistically significant reductions in fasting blood glucose and glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c), though effect sizes are generally smaller than those of standard oral hypoglycaemics. Traditional systems (Ayurveda, Traditional Chinese Medicine, Caribbean and African folk medicine) also employ the fruit as an anthelmintic [against intestinal worms], emmenagogue [to stimulate menstruation], antiviral, and general tonic; these uses have preliminary in vitro and animal-model support but lack robust clinical trial evidence.

PHARMACOLOGY — In vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of α-glucosidase and α-amylase (contributing to reduced postprandial glucose), as well as antiviral activity of MAP30 and momorcharins against HIV-1 and HSV. In vivo rodent studies confirm dose-dependent hypoglycaemic effects and hepatoprotective activity. Antitumour effects (apoptosis induction, cell-cycle arrest) have been documented in cancer cell lines but have not been demonstrated clinically.

ADVERSE REACTIONS — Gastrointestinal disturbance (nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhoea) is the most common adverse effect. Hypoglycaemic episodes may occur, particularly in combination with insulin or oral hypoglycaemic drugs. Vicine and convicine content may precipitate haemolytic anaemia in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD)-deficient individuals. Seeds and latex are abortifacient in animal models.

CONTRAINDICATIONS — Pregnancy (abortifacient risk); G6PD deficiency; paediatric use without medical supervision; concurrent use with insulin or sulfonylureas without blood glucose monitoring.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE SUMMARY — (1) A randomised controlled trial (Fuangchan et al., 2011, n=129, type 2 DM) found 2000 mg/day dried fruit extract produced a modest but significant reduction in fructosamine compared to placebo, though inferior to 5 mg/day glibenclamide. (2) A Cochrane-style systematic review (Ooi et al., 2012) identified four RCTs; all showed directional improvement in glycaemic markers but were limited by small sample sizes, short durations, and heterogeneous preparations. (3) Animal-model mechanistic studies (in vivo, streptozotocin-induced diabetic rats) consistently confirm activation of AMPK and PPAR-γ pathways as central to the hypoglycaemic mechanism.

entities_mentioned.name: Momordica charantia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bitter melon / bitter gourd — Momordica charantia L., family Cucurbitaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated pantropical species; the unripe fruit (Fructus Momordicae Charantiae) is the official plant part per WHO monograph. Synonyms include karela (Hindi), ku gua (Chinese), cerasee (Caribbean).

entities_mentioned.name: Charantin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal saponin mixture: β-sitosterol-3-O-β-D-glucopyranoside and stigmasterol-3-O-β-D-glucopyranoside (1:1)
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered a primary hypoglycaemic constituent; acts partly by stimulating pancreatic β-cell insulin release.

entities_mentioned.name: Polypeptide-p
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plant-derived insulin-like polypeptide (~17 amino acids, MW ~11 kDa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically called 'plant insulin'; subcutaneous administration lowers blood glucose in animal models; oral bioavailability is uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: MAP30
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Momordica anti-HIV protein 30 kDa — a type I ribosome-inactivating protein (RIP)
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated antiviral activity against HIV-1 and HSV in vitro; also shows antitumour properties. Present mainly in seeds and fruit latex.

entities_mentioned.name: Vicine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pyrimidine nucleoside glycoside (divicine precursor); found also in fava beans
entities_mentioned.notes: Clinically relevant: may trigger haemolytic anaemia in G6PD-deficient patients, analogous to favism.

entities_mentioned.name: α-Eleostearic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Conjugated octadecatrienoic acid (18:3 Δ9c,11t,13t); major fatty acid of bitter melon seed oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Reported to induce apoptosis in cancer cell lines in vitro; ~57% of seed oil fatty acid fraction.

entities_mentioned.name: AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cellular energy-sensing enzyme; key target in type 2 diabetes pharmacology
entities_mentioned.notes: Activation by bitter melon constituents is a well-supported mechanistic finding in rodent and cell-culture studies; same pathway targeted by metformin.
