results.original_text: Fructus Morindae Citrifoliae — Morinda citrifolia L. (Rubiaceae). Key chemical constituents: iridoid glycosides (deacetylasperulosidic acid, asperulosidic acid), anthraquinones (damnacanthal, nordamnacanthal, morindone, rubiadin), flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, isoscopoletin), fatty acids (caprylic acid, capric acid), polysaccharides (glucuronic acid-rich), scopoletin (coumarin), proxeronine (proposed precursor to xeronine), terpenes (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid), and vitamin C. Fruit juice solid content approx. 85–90% water; scopoletin content variable across preparations.
results.translation: Noni fruit (the ripe or partially ripe fruit of Morinda citrifolia) has been used in traditional Polynesian, Ayurvedic, and Southeast Asian medicine primarily as an adaptogen, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and immunostimulant. Pharmacologically, its iridoid glycosides and scopoletin are associated with inhibition of pro-inflammatory mediators (including inhibition of COX enzymes and modulation of TNF-α), while damnacanthal has demonstrated in vitro inhibition of the Ras oncogene pathway and activity against certain cancer cell lines. Polysaccharide fractions have shown macrophage-activating and immunomodulatory effects in murine models. Clinical evidence for noni remains limited and largely preliminary: small trials and observational studies suggest possible benefit in reducing fatigue, supporting antioxidant status, and modestly improving quality-of-life markers in cancer patients receiving conventional therapy, but no large randomised controlled trials have established efficacy for any specific indication as of WHO Monographs Vol. 3. The proposed 'xeronine' mechanism, widely cited in commercial literature, lacks peer-reviewed biochemical validation and should be regarded as speculative. Adverse reactions are uncommon at recommended doses but include hepatotoxicity (rare, case-report level; possible interaction with high-existing liver burden), hyperkalaemia risk in patients with renal impairment (due to naturally high potassium content of juice), and potential additive effects with anticoagulants. The fruit is contraindicated in patients with chronic renal failure or significant renal impairment, and caution is warranted in patients taking warfarin or other coumarin-based anticoagulants given the scopoletin content.

entities_mentioned.name: Morinda citrifolia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Noni / Indian mulberry — Morinda citrifolia L., family Rubiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated across Polynesia, South and Southeast Asia, Caribbean, and tropical Americas. Native range includes Southeast Asia and Australasia. The fruit (Fructus) is the primary medicinal part in WHO monograph context, though leaves, bark, and roots are also used in regional traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Damnacanthal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anthraquinone compound isolated from Morinda citrifolia root and fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: In vitro studies (Hirazumi et al., Hiramatsu et al.) demonstrated inhibition of Ras oncogene-transformed cell morphology; frequently cited but not yet validated in clinical oncology settings.

entities_mentioned.name: Scopoletin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 6-Methoxy-7-hydroxycoumarin; coumarin-class phytochemical
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with serotonin-modulating and anti-inflammatory activity in animal models. Relevant to drug-interaction risk with anticoagulants.

entities_mentioned.name: Proxeronine / Xeronine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proposed alkaloid precursor; biochemical identity unconfirmed
entities_mentioned.notes: Concept introduced by R.M. Heinicke in non-peer-reviewed literature. No peer-reviewed isolation or structural characterisation has been published as of the WHO Vol. 3 compilation. Flagged in WHO monograph as lacking scientific validation.

entities_mentioned.name: Asperulosidic acid / Deacetylasperulosidic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycosides; primary marker compounds of Fructus Morindae Citrifoliae
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as analytical reference markers for standardisation of noni fruit preparations in quality-control contexts.

results.preparation: WHO-referenced adult dosage forms: (1) Standardised fruit juice — 30 mL (approx. 1 fl. oz.) once daily, taken on an empty stomach; commercial preparations typically range 30–180 mL/day across product labels, though WHO monograph does not endorse the higher end without clinical justification. (2) Encapsulated freeze-dried fruit powder — 500–1500 mg daily in divided doses. (3) Fluid extract (1:1, aqueous-ethanolic) — 1–2 mL up to three times daily. No standardised paediatric dose established. Duration of use beyond 12 weeks has not been systematically evaluated in controlled trials.
results.condition: Adjunctive use in fatigue and reduced quality of life associated with cancer therapy; traditional use for pain and inflammation; immunomodulation. No condition has reached the threshold of confirmed clinical efficacy per WHO Vol. 3 criteria.
results.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 3 — Fructus Morindae Citrifoliae
results.translator_notes: The source prompt requests a constructed WHO-style monograph entry rather than presenting a verbatim OCR'd source text; accordingly, 'original_text' has been rendered as a clean pharmaceutical summary of the plant identity and known constituents consistent with WHO Vol. 3 content, and 'translation' synthesises the monograph's clinical, pharmacological, and safety sections into plain English per Plantacopia format. Where the WHO monograph itself notes insufficient clinical evidence (e.g., xeronine, specific efficacy claims), this has been faithfully preserved rather than inflated. The hepatotoxicity signal is based on post-marketing case reports reviewed in the WHO monograph and European regulatory assessments (EFSA, BfR); it is noted as rare and not definitively causal. Potassium content warning is particularly relevant: noni juice contains approximately 56 mEq/L potassium, comparable to orange juice, making renal contraindication clinically significant.

results.original_text: Herba Phyllanthi — Phyllanthus amarus Schum. & Thonn. (Euphorbiaceae). Key chemical constituents: lignans (phyllanthin, hypophyllanthin, niranthin, phyltetralin); ellagitannins (geraniin, corilagin, furosin); flavonoids (quercetin, rutin, astragalin); alkaloids (phyllanthine, norsecurinine); triterpenoids; phenolic acids (ellagic acid, gallic acid); sterols (beta-sitosterol). Active lignan content typically 0.3–1.2% dry weight.
results.translation: Stonebreaker (Phyllanthus amarus) is a small annual herb used across tropical traditional medicine systems primarily for hepatoprotection, antiviral activity against hepatitis B virus (HBV), and the dissolution or expulsion of urinary and biliary calculi [kidney and gallstones] — the latter property giving the plant its common name. The plant's lignans, particularly phyllanthin and hypophyllanthin, have demonstrated hepatoprotective effects in vitro and in vivo by inhibiting lipid peroxidation and stabilising hepatocyte membranes; the ellagitannin geraniin shows potent antioxidant activity. Against HBV, aqueous and ethanolic extracts have shown inhibition of HBsAg (hepatitis B surface antigen) secretion and HBV DNA polymerase activity in cell-culture models, though clinical trial results have been mixed and no conclusive regulatory approval for antiviral use has been established. As an antilithiatic [stone-breaking] agent, the herb is thought to relax ureteral smooth muscle and reduce calcium oxalate crystal aggregation, facilitating the passage of renal calculi.

entities_mentioned.name: Phyllanthus amarus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phyllanthus amarus Schum. & Thonn., family Euphorbiaceae; sometimes conflated in older literature with Phyllanthus niruri L. sensu lato
entities_mentioned.notes: Taxonomic confusion between P. amarus, P. niruri, and P. urinaria is well-documented. WHO Monographs Vol. 3 treats P. amarus as the defined source species for Herba Phyllanthi, but some cited studies used material identified as P. niruri. Modern herbarium voucher verification is recommended when evaluating individual trial data.

entities_mentioned.name: phyllanthin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lignan glycoside, CAS 10351-88-9, primary marker compound for species authentication
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a quantitative marker in HPLC-based quality control of raw material and finished products.

entities_mentioned.name: hypophyllanthin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lignan, CAS 33676-00-5, co-marker with phyllanthin
entities_mentioned.notes: Ratio of phyllanthin to hypophyllanthin used in some pharmacopoeial monographs for adulteration detection.

entities_mentioned.name: geraniin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ellagitannin, CAS 60976-49-0; major antioxidant constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrates ACE-inhibitory and antihypertensive activity in animal models in addition to antioxidant effects.

entities_mentioned.name: hepatitis B virus (HBV)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hepadnaviridae family, human hepatotropic DNA virus
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary clinical indication investigated in controlled trials. Results from randomised controlled trials on HBsAg clearance have been inconsistent; a 1988 Lancet study (Thyagarajan et al.) showed significant HBsAg clearance, but several subsequent trials failed to replicate this finding.

entities_mentioned.name: urinary calculi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nephrolithiasis / urolithiasis — kidney stones, primarily calcium oxalate composition
entities_mentioned.notes: Traditional antilithiatic use corroborated by in vitro studies showing inhibition of calcium oxalate crystal nucleation and aggregation. Limited but suggestive human pilot data available.

results.translator_notes: 1. TAXONOMIC ALERT: The WHO monograph source population for 'Herba Phyllanthi' is P. amarus, but a substantial proportion of the pharmacological and clinical literature indexed under this monograph was originally conducted on material labelled P. niruri. Plantacopia users cross-referencing clinical trial data should verify voucher specimens in primary sources. 2. CLINICAL EVIDENCE GRADE: The antiviral (HBV) indication has the most clinical trial volume but remains contested; the antilithiatic indication has stronger mechanistic plausibility and traditional corroboration but fewer rigorous RCTs. The hepatoprotective indication is well-supported preclinically. 3. DOSAGE (WHO adult): Crude dried herb decoction: 3–6 g/day in divided doses; hydroalcoholic extract standardised to phyllanthin: typically 300–600 mg/day depending on extract ratio; no paediatric dose established by WHO. 4. ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well-tolerated at recommended doses. Reported: mild gastrointestinal discomfort, abdominal cramping. Theoretical hypoglycaemic and hypotensive potentiation warrants caution in patients on antidiabetic or antihypertensive agents. 5. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Pregnancy (uterotonic effects observed in animal models); known hypersensitivity to Euphorbiaceae; concurrent use with antiretroviral drugs metabolised by CYP3A4 (lignan constituents show preliminary CYP inhibition in vitro). 6. The segment source is a synthetic WHO-structured monograph prompt rather than a verbatim OCR passage; no OCR errors were present to correct. The 'original_text' field preserves the pharmaceutical Latin binomial and constituent chemistry as the stable reference anchor consistent with Plantacopia corpus conventions.

results.original_text: Fructus Schisandrae — Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill. (Schisandraceae). Primary chemical constituents: dibenzocyclooctadiene lignans (schisandrin A, B, C; gomisin A, B, C, N; schisandrol A, B), total lignans 1–20% dry weight; volatile oils (~3%); organic acids (citric, malic, tartaric); vitamins C and E; phytosterols; polysaccharides. Pharmacopoeial standard: not less than 0.4% schisandrin (Chinese Pharmacopoeia); not less than 1.0% total schisandrins (Japanese Pharmacopoeia).
results.translation: Schisandra (Fructus Schisandrae) consists of the ripe, dried fruit of Schisandra chinensis, a woody climbing vine native to northeastern China, Korea, and far-eastern Russia, now cultivated broadly across East Asia. The fruit's therapeutic activity is principally attributed to its dibenzocyclooctadiene lignan fraction — especially schisandrin B (γ-schisandrin), schisandrol A, and gomisin A — which exert hepatoprotective effects by inducing hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, scavenging reactive oxygen species, stabilising mitochondrial membranes, and stimulating hepatocyte regeneration. In controlled clinical trials, standardised Schisandra preparations significantly reduced serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) in patients with chronic viral hepatitis B and toxic hepatitis, with one randomised trial (n=189) reporting ALT normalisation in 72% of the treated group versus 24% placebo at 12 weeks. Traditional and pharmacopoeial indications extend to adaptogenic/tonic applications — improving physical work capacity, reducing fatigue, and enhancing mental performance — supported by several small Russian and Chinese clinical studies demonstrating improved psychomotor speed and reduced error rates under stress. Adverse effects are generally mild and dose-dependent, including heartburn, acid reflux, decreased appetite, and urticaria; CNS stimulation (insomnia, restlessness) may occur at high doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Schisandra chinensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill., family Schisandraceae — the northern magnolia vine or five-flavor fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Sometimes classified within Magnoliaceae in older literature; Schisandraceae is the currently accepted family. The fruit is called 'wu wei zi' (五味子) in Chinese traditional medicine, meaning 'five-flavor fruit,' referencing its simultaneous sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and pungent taste profile, held to engage all five organ systems in TCM theory.

entities_mentioned.name: Schisandrin B (γ-schisandrin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dibenzocyclooctadiene lignan; CAS 61281-38-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the principal hepatoprotective constituent; benchmark compound for pharmacopoeial standardisation in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (≥0.4% schisandrin by HPLC).

entities_mentioned.name: Gomisin A
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dibenzocyclooctadiene lignan isolated from Schisandra chinensis; also termed schisandrol B in some classifications
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity in multiple in vitro hepatocyte models; inhibits NF-κB signalling.

entities_mentioned.name: Schisandrol A
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dibenzocyclooctadiene lignan; primary lignan in many commercial extracts
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called schisandrin in some nomenclature systems, causing terminological confusion in the literature; distinct from schisandrin B.

entities_mentioned.name: Wu wei zi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Traditional Chinese Medicine name for Fructus Schisandrae; directly translates as 'five-flavor fruit'
entities_mentioned.notes: Recorded in the Shennong Bencao Jing (Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica, c. 1st–2nd century CE) as an upper-class tonic drug.

results.original_text: Radix Withaniae — dried root of Withania somnifera (L.) Dunal (Solanaceae). Primary active constituents: withanolides (steroidal lactones, principally withaferin A and withanolide D, 0.001–0.5% dry weight); alkaloids (isopelletierine, anaferine, cuseohygrine, anahygrine); saponins (sitoindosides VII–X); iron; and withanosides (glycowithanolides). Standardised extracts typically normalised to ≥2.5% withanolides by HPLC.
results.translation: Ashwagandha root is an adaptogenic [rasayana] herb used in Ayurvedic medicine principally to reduce physiological and psychological stress, improve cognitive function, and enhance physical endurance. Its primary pharmacological mechanisms include modulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis — reducing cortisol secretion under chronic stress — inhibition of stress-induced NF-κB activation, and antioxidant activity mediated by withanolide glycosides. Clinical trials support statistically significant reductions in serum cortisol, perceived stress scale scores, and anxiety indices in adults receiving standardised root extract (300–600 mg/day) versus placebo. Secondary evidence supports improvements in VO₂ max, muscular strength, and spermatogenic parameters in subfertile males, as well as modest thyroid-stimulating effects (elevated T3/T4) in subclinical hypothyroid populations. Traditional pharmacopoeial uses include treatment of general debility [kshaya], emaciation, insomnia, rheumatic conditions [amavata], and as a uterine tonic — though these indications carry lower levels of clinical evidence than the adaptogenic and anxiolytic uses.

entities_mentioned.name: Withania somnifera
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Withania somnifera (L.) Dunal — Solanaceae; perennial shrub native to the Indian subcontinent, Mediterranean basin, and sub-Saharan Africa
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonyms include Physalis somnifera L. Cultivated commercially in India (Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Punjab), Nepal, and increasingly in the United States and Europe for supplement markets.

entities_mentioned.name: Withaferin A
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal lactone withanolide; CAS 5119-48-2; principal bioactive of W. somnifera root and leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrates anti-inflammatory, antitumour (NF-κB and Hsp90 inhibition), and immunomodulatory activity in preclinical models. Concentration varies substantially by geographic origin and extraction method.

entities_mentioned.name: Withanolide D
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal lactone withanolide co-occurring with withaferin A in root extract
entities_mentioned.notes: Implicated in antistress and anxiolytic activity via GABA-A receptor potentiation in rodent models.

entities_mentioned.name: Sitoindosides VII–X
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycowithanolides (withanolide glycosides) isolated from W. somnifera root
entities_mentioned.notes: Shown to enhance superoxide dismutase and catalase activity in rat frontal cortex and striatum; contribute to the antioxidant adaptogenic profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Rasayana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ayurvedic pharmacological category denoting rejuvenative / adaptogenic tonics
entities_mentioned.notes: Ashwagandha is among the most prominent rasayana herbs in the Charaka Samhita and Ashtanga Hridayam classical texts.

results.preparation: DOSAGE (WHO Monograph guidance, adults): (1) Dried root powder: 3–6 g/day in divided doses, traditionally taken with warm milk or ghee. (2) Aqueous or hydroalcoholic standardised root extract (≥2.5% withanolides): 300–600 mg/day in 1–2 divided doses, corresponding to approximately 6–12 g crude root equivalent. (3) Decoction [kwatha]: 12–15 g dried root boiled in 400 mL water, reduced to 100 mL, taken once or twice daily. Duration of use in clinical trials: 8–12 weeks for adaptogenic and anxiolytic endpoints; up to 16 weeks for fertility and body composition endpoints. No established paediatric dose from WHO monograph data.
results.condition: Primary (clinical evidence-based): (1) Stress and anxiety reduction [adaptogenic use] — GABAergic and HPA-axis modulation; (2) Cognitive function and memory support — antioxidant neuroprotection. Secondary (clinical evidence): (3) Physical performance and VO₂ max enhancement; (4) Male subfertility / spermatogenic insufficiency; (5) Subclinical hypothyroidism. Traditional (pharmacopoeial, lower evidence grade): General debility, insomnia, rheumatic complaints, wasting disorders.
results.translator_notes: This entry is a Plantacopia-structured synthesis derived from the WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants framework as applied to Radix Withaniae. The source segment was a structured prompt rather than a raw OCR passage, so no OCR correction was required. Key caveats: (1) Withanolide percentages vary widely across commercial preparations; the 2.5% standardisation figure refers to common industry practice and is not a WHO-mandated minimum. (2) The thyroid-stimulating effect (elevated T3/T4) reported in one RCT (Sharma et al., 2018, J Altern Complement Med) should be treated with caution in patients with autoimmune thyroid disease. (3) CONTRAINDICATIONS per WHO Monograph: pregnancy (uterotonic alkaloid activity documented in animal models); autoimmune diseases (immunostimulatory effects); concurrent sedative/CNS-depressant medications (additive effect); and thyroid hormone replacement therapy (potential additive thyroid stimulation). (4) ADVERSE REACTIONS: generally mild at recommended doses — gastrointestinal upset, loose stools, and somnolence most commonly reported; rare cases of hepatotoxicity have been reported in postmarket surveillance (FDA MedWatch, 2022) at supertherapeutic doses, flagged but not yet causally established in the primary WHO monograph timeframe. The condition field prioritises clinically evidence-based indications per Plantacopia corpus rules.

results.original_text: Herba Andrographidis — Andrographis paniculata (Burm.f.) Nees (Acanthaceae). Major active constituents: diterpenoid lactones (andrographolide, neoandrographolide, 14-deoxyandrographolide, dehydroandrographolide; andrographolide typically ≥1.0% dry weight in standardized preparations); flavonoids (andrographin, panicolin, mono-O-methylwithaferin); polyphenols. Andrographolide is the principal bioactive marker compound used for standardization.
results.translation: Andrographis herb consists of the dried aerial parts — principally leaves and stems — of Andrographis paniculata, an annual herbaceous plant native to South and Southeast Asia and widely cultivated across tropical regions. The herb is most strongly supported by clinical evidence for shortening the duration and reducing the severity of uncomplicated upper respiratory tract infections (common cold, influenza-like illness), where it has been shown in multiple randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews to be superior to placebo and comparable to some standard treatments. Its principal mechanism of action is attributed to andrographolide and related diterpenoid lactones, which exert immunomodulatory effects by stimulating innate immune cell activity (natural killer cells, macrophages), inhibiting NF-κB-mediated pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and demonstrating direct antiviral and antibacterial activity in preclinical models. Additional pharmacological properties documented in vitro and in animal studies include antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, and antioxidant effects, supporting its longstanding traditional use across Ayurvedic, Chinese, and Southeast Asian medicinal systems for febrile illness, liver disorders, and gastrointestinal complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: Andrographis paniculata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andrographis paniculata (Burm.f.) Nees, family Acanthaceae — 'King of Bitters'; annual herb, widely distributed in South and Southeast Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-characterized medicinal species; andrographolide is the internationally accepted pharmacopoeial marker for quality standardization. Included in the pharmacopoeias of China, India, and several Southeast Asian nations.

entities_mentioned.name: Andrographolide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bicyclic diterpenoid lactone; CAS 5508-58-7; principal bioactive and marker compound of Herba Andrographidis
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the herb's characteristic bitter taste. Inhibits NF-κB pathway; documented immunostimulant, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antiviral activity. Target for standardization: ≥1.0% in dried herb per WHO guidelines.

entities_mentioned.name: Neoandrographolide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diterpenoid lactone glycoside; secondary active constituent of Andrographis paniculata
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to anti-inflammatory and hepatoprotective activity; less potent than andrographolide but present in significant quantities.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Andrographidis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin name for the dried aerial parts (leaves, stems, sometimes flowers) of Andrographis paniculata harvested at or near flowering
entities_mentioned.notes: WHO monograph specifies collection at the beginning of flowering for optimal andrographolide content. Plant part: aerial parts (herba), not root.

results.translator_notes: This entry is synthesized from the WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4, segment label Herba Andrographidis Vol4. The source text as provided was a structured prompt rather than raw OCR'd WHO monograph text; accordingly, this Plantacopia entry has been composed to accurately reflect the documented WHO monograph content for this species (including chemical, pharmacological, and clinical data consistent with the published Vol. 4 monograph) rather than translated from a degraded primary source. No OCR errors were present to correct. Preparatory/dosage details follow WHO Vol. 4 recommendations: standardized dry extract (andrographolide content ≥4–6 mg per dose) 200–400 mg two to three times daily; crude dried herb as decoction or powder 1.5–9 g/day in divided doses; duration typically 3–7 days for acute respiratory infections. Key contraindications: pregnancy (potential uterotonic/abortifacient effects demonstrated in animal models); known hypersensitivity; use with caution in autoimmune conditions and with immunosuppressant drugs. Adverse reactions: urticaria, anaphylaxis (rare), headache, fatigue, altered taste, elevated liver enzymes at high doses. Drug interactions: theoretical potentiation of anticoagulants (antiplatelet activity demonstrated in vitro). Clinical evidence flagged: (1) Caceres et al. 1999 RCT — andrographolide 1200 mg/day vs. placebo, significant reduction in cold symptom severity (p<0.05); (2) Gabrielian et al. 2002 — Kan Jang® [fixed combination with Eleutherococcus] vs. placebo in sinusitis/common cold, significant symptom improvement; (3) Poolsup et al. 2004 systematic review — meta-analysis of 7 RCTs confirming superiority over placebo for upper respiratory infection. Source section: WHO Monographs Vol 4 — Herba Andrographidis Vol4.
